past_medical_history: Distal esophageal adenocarcinoma, status post neoadjuvant chemotherapy and en bloc esophagectomy with cervical esophagostomy, pyloroplasty, and feeding-tube placement 3 weeks ago
Atrial fibrillation, diagnosed 5 years ago, previously on apixaban but discontinued 4 weeks ago perioperatively and not yet restarted
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 6 years ago

medications.indication: Atrial fibrillation rate control and hypertension

family_history: Father had a stroke at age 70
Mother had type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 25 pack-year history, quit 5 years ago at cancer diagnosis
social_history.alcohol: Former moderate drinker, 1-2 drinks per day; stopped after cancer diagnosis
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 1 mile daily, now limited by postoperative recovery

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but confused. Oriented to self and place, not to date. Decreased initiation of speech. Follows simple commands but slow to respond. Attention impaired on serial 7s. Unable to recall 3 objects at 5 minutes (0/3).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive to light. Left homonymous hemianopsia on confrontation visual field testing. No ptosis. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. No facial droop. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 grip, 4-/5 deltoid, 4/5 triceps and biceps. Left lower extremity: 4/5 hip flexion, 4+/5 knee extension, 4/5 ankle dorsiflexion. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased pinprick and light touch sensation on the left arm and leg. Left-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation in visual and tactile modalities. Proprioception intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 3+ with equivocal plantar response on the left. Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly impaired on the left with slight past-pointing. Right finger-to-nose intact. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based. Tends to bump into objects on the left side. Requires assistance for ambulation.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 8. Left hemineglect on line bisection and clock drawing tasks. No meningismus. Cachexia noted with recent 20-lb weight loss.

patient.chief_complaint: New confusion, left visual field loss, and left-sided weakness found during acute rehabilitation assessment
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Robert Kessler is a 67-year-old right-handed man with a history of distal esophageal adenocarcinoma (status post neoadjuvant chemotherapy and en bloc esophagectomy three weeks ago), atrial fibrillation (anticoagulation discontinued perioperatively and not restarted), hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia, who was transferred emergently from the acute rehabilitation unit after nursing staff noted new neurological deficits during a routine morning assessment. The patient had been admitted to the rehabilitation facility two days ago following a prolonged postoperative hospital course complicated by a brief episode of aspiration pneumonia that has since resolved. On the morning of transfer, the rehabilitation nurse noticed the patient was having difficulty navigating the left side of his breakfast tray and repeatedly bumped into the doorframe on his left side when attempting to walk to the bathroom. When questioned, the patient seemed confused and was slow to respond, though he had been alert and conversant the evening prior. The overnight nursing notes documented that the patient slept through the night without any reported events, and the last documented neurological check at 22:00 the night before was unremarkable. His wife, who was visiting in the morning, confirmed that he had been at his postoperative baseline the previous evening, engaging in conversation and watching television without difficulty. She noted that when she arrived at 07:30 this morning, he appeared confused, was not tracking objects to his left, and had difficulty using his left hand to hold his coffee cup. There was no report of headache, seizure activity, nausea, vomiting, or fever. Given his history of esophageal cancer, the rehabilitation team was concerned about possible brain metastasis versus a new stroke, particularly in light of his discontinued anticoagulation, and activated a stroke alert for emergent transfer to the emergency department. He arrived in the ED at 08:45. His last known well time is established at approximately 22:00 the night before, roughly 10.5 hours prior to symptom recognition.

findings.location: Right parietal-occipital lobe involving the right posterior cerebral artery and right MCA posterior division watershed territory
findings.size: Approximately 5.2 x 3.8 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Markedly hyperintense signal indicating restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal consistent with infarct older than 4-6 hours
signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense signal in the affected territory
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the right parietal-occipital region
signal_characteristics.Post-contrast T1: No abnormal enhancement. No ring enhancement or nodular enhancement to suggest neoplasm

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement in the right parietal-occipital region. No midline shift. No uncal herniation.
findings.borders: Well-defined margins conforming to a vascular territory distribution involving right PCA and posterior MCA watershed zone

findings.location: Right posterior cerebral artery P2 segment

signal_characteristics.MRA: Diminished flow signal in the right PCA P2 segment with reduced distal branch opacification
signal_characteristics.TOF: Decreased flow-related enhancement in the right PCA territory

additional_observations: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease: no enhancing parenchymal lesions, no leptomeningeal enhancement, no dural-based masses
Scattered periventricular white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas grade 2)
No hemorrhagic transformation within the infarct territory
Bilateral carotid and vertebral arteries patent on MRA of the neck
No restricted diffusion elsewhere to suggest additional acute embolic events
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss appropriate for age
mri.impression: 1. Acute right parietal-occipital infarction with restricted diffusion conforming to a vascular territory distribution (right PCA and posterior MCA watershed). The morphology, vascular territory distribution, DWI/ADC signal pattern, and absence of enhancement are characteristic of acute ischemic stroke and inconsistent with brain metastasis or primary brain neoplasm. 2. Diminished flow in the right PCA P2 segment on MRA suggesting partial or complete occlusion. 3. No intracranial metastatic disease identified. 4. Chronic small vessel ischemic changes.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right PCA territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction with ADC confirmation in a vascular territory distribution, absence of enhancement, vascular occlusion on MRA
No ring enhancement, no nodular enhancement, lesion follows vascular territory rather than appearing as a round mass, no surrounding vasogenic edema pattern typical of metastasis
No enhancement, no infiltrative pattern, lesion respects vascular territory boundaries

recommended_actions: Cardiology consultation for atrial fibrillation management and anticoagulation timing
CTA head and neck for further vascular characterization
Follow-up MRI in 5-7 days to assess for hemorrhagic transformation
Echocardiogram to evaluate for cardioembolic source

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in inferolateral leads
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 104 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. Nonspecific ST-T wave changes. No acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Atrial fibrillation in a patient whose anticoagulation was discontinued perioperatively is a well-established source of cardioembolic stroke. Rate control should be optimized. Restoration of anticoagulation should be planned once acute stroke management is completed.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild anemia, likely multifactorial: recent major surgery, chronic disease related to malignancy, and nutritional deficiency
Low RBC count consistent with anemia

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely prerenal from poor oral intake postoperatively
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes and acute physiological stress

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Normal INR confirms patient is not therapeutically anticoagulated

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Above target for patient with vascular risk factors

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Suboptimally controlled diabetes, significant stroke risk factor
Low albumin consistent with malnutrition and recent major surgery; contributes to poor wound healing and postoperative risk
Within normal limits, reassuring in the context of recent esophageal cancer treatment

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results reveal normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.2) consistent with recent major surgery and chronic disease. Mildly elevated BUN suggests mild dehydration. Hyperglycemia consistent with known diabetes and stress response. Coagulation studies are normal, confirming the patient is not therapeutically anticoagulated despite his atrial fibrillation history. Lipid panel shows suboptimal control despite statin use. Low albumin reflects nutritional compromise following esophagectomy. HbA1c indicates suboptimally controlled diabetes. CEA within normal limits.

findings.location: Right posterior cerebral artery P2 segment

signal_characteristics.CTA: Near-complete occlusion of the right PCA P2 segment with minimal residual flow distally. Likely embolic thrombus.

findings.location: Bilateral carotid bifurcations and vertebrobasilar system

signal_characteristics.CTA: Mild calcified plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations with less than 30% stenosis. Mild atheromatous changes of the basilar artery. Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with normal caliber.

additional_observations: Bilateral internal carotid arteries patent without significant stenosis
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with normal caliber
Basilar artery patent with mild atherosclerotic changes
Left PCA and bilateral MCAs and ACAs are patent
No evidence of dissection in cervical or intracranial vessels
Fetal-type right PCA variant noted
output.impression: 1. Near-complete occlusion of the right PCA P2 segment, most consistent with cardioembolic thrombus given the clinical history of atrial fibrillation. 2. Mild atherosclerotic disease of the carotid bifurcations and basilar artery without hemodynamically significant stenosis. 3. No cervical or intracranial vessel dissection.
recommended_actions: Anticoagulation planning for cardioembolic stroke secondary to atrial fibrillation
Consider transesophageal echocardiogram if transthoracic is inconclusive

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderately dilated left atrium, consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation and elevated cardioembolic risk
Likely functional due to atrial and annular dilation
Small and likely incidental; may be related to recent surgery or hypoalbuminemia

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates mildly reduced LVEF (52%), concentric LVH consistent with chronic hypertension, and moderate left atrial dilation consistent with longstanding atrial fibrillation. Mild to moderate mitral regurgitation present. Trace pericardial effusion likely related to hypoalbuminemia and recent surgery. The dilated left atrium in the setting of atrial fibrillation represents a significant cardioembolic risk and is the most likely mechanism for the patient's stroke.

findings: Persistent atrial fibrillation throughout the 24-hour recording period
No periods of sinus rhythm identified
Episodes of rapid ventricular response >130 bpm totaling approximately 2.5 hours, predominantly during daytime activity
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (68 in 24 hours, <1% burden)
No sustained or non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals persistent atrial fibrillation with suboptimal rate control (average rate 92 bpm with episodes of RVR). No pauses or significant ventricular arrhythmias.
output.clinical_correlation: Persistent atrial fibrillation confirmed. Suboptimal rate control with intermittent rapid ventricular response should be addressed by uptitrating beta-blocker or adding digoxin. Anticoagulation is critical for secondary stroke prevention.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Elevated, expected in postoperative state and with active cancer history; not specific for thrombophilia
Mildly elevated, may reflect nutritional deficiency (B12/folate) in setting of esophagectomy and reduced oral intake

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel shows no hereditary or acquired thrombophilia. Elevated D-dimer is expected postoperatively and in the context of recent cancer treatment. Mildly elevated homocysteine may reflect nutritional deficiency related to impaired absorption after esophagectomy. The stroke etiology is most consistent with cardioembolism from atrial fibrillation without additional prothrombotic contribution.

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation
Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate normal flow velocities
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with antegrade flow
Mild calcified plaque at bilateral carotid bifurcations
output.impression: 1. Mild bilateral carotid bifurcation atherosclerotic disease with less than 40% stenosis bilaterally. 2. No hemodynamically significant carotid stenosis. 3. Patent bilateral vertebral arteries. The degree of carotid disease is insufficient to account for the stroke; cardioembolic mechanism from atrial fibrillation remains the most likely etiology.
recommended_actions: Medical optimization of atherosclerotic risk factors
Repeat carotid duplex in 12 months for surveillance
Focus on anticoagulation for cardioembolic stroke prevention

output.query: ischemic stroke management in cancer patients anticoagulation perioperative

results.title: Stroke and Cancer: A Review of Risk, Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Management
results.authors: Dearborn JL, Urrutia VC, Zeiler SR
results.key_finding: Cancer patients have increased stroke risk through multiple mechanisms including hypercoagulability, nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis, and tumor embolism. Standard stroke treatments apply but cancer-specific considerations are necessary.

results.title: Perioperative Stroke and the Role of Atrial Fibrillation
results.authors: Mashour GA, Shanks AM, Kheterpal S
results.key_finding: Perioperative stroke risk is highest in the first 2 weeks postoperatively. Patients with atrial fibrillation whose anticoagulation is interrupted have significantly elevated risk.

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase recommended within 4.5 hours of onset. For wake-up strokes or unknown onset, DWI-FLAIR mismatch may be used to identify patients who may benefit from thrombolysis.

output.summary: Cancer patients face elevated stroke risk, particularly in the perioperative period when anticoagulation is interrupted. For patients with known atrial fibrillation, perioperative anticoagulation management is critical. In the acute stroke setting, standard treatments (thrombolysis, thrombectomy) apply unless specific contraindications exist (e.g., recent major surgery within 14 days for tPA). For patients beyond the IV tPA window, thrombectomy should be considered for LVO. Secondary prevention in cancer patients with AF should include anticoagulation, with DOAC preferred unless cancer-specific indications favor LMWH.

output.proposed: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily for secondary stroke prevention in patient with atrial fibrillation, recent esophagectomy, and active cancer history
interactions: Pantoprazole: minimal interaction, no dose adjustment required
Enoxaparin: must discontinue prophylactic dose before starting therapeutic anticoagulation to avoid dual anticoagulant effect
contraindications: Recent major surgery (esophagectomy 3 weeks ago): relative contraindication to full-dose anticoagulation in the early postoperative period; consult surgery regarding surgical site hemostasis
Active cancer: consider cancer-associated thrombosis guidelines; DOACs generally acceptable for AF-related stroke prevention even in cancer patients
warnings: Risk of GI bleeding is elevated given recent esophageal surgery and anastomotic healing
Low albumin (2.8 g/dL) may affect drug protein binding but clinically insignificant for apixaban
Monitor for signs of bleeding given recent surgical status
Anemia (Hgb 11.2) — monitor hemoglobin serially after initiating anticoagulation
output.formulary_status: Preferred formulary, prior authorization not required
alternatives: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily (higher GI bleeding risk, less preferred post-esophagectomy)
Warfarin with INR monitoring (more adjustable, but drug-food interactions problematic in patient with altered GI anatomy)
Low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin therapeutic dose) as bridge until surgical site is fully healed

findings.location: Right parietal-occipital lobe (same as prior study)

signal_characteristics.Post-contrast T1: No abnormal enhancement within the infarct. No ring-enhancing or nodular-enhancing lesions anywhere in the brain parenchyma.
signal_characteristics.SWI: No susceptibility artifact to suggest hemorrhagic transformation or underlying hemorrhagic metastasis
signal_characteristics.3D_volumetric_T1_post_contrast: Comprehensive review of all slices shows no enhancing lesions suspicious for metastatic disease

findings.mass_effect: Mild sulcal effacement in right parietal-occipital region, unchanged

additional_observations: Dedicated metastatic protocol with thin-section post-contrast 3D T1-weighted imaging shows no enhancing parenchymal lesions
No leptomeningeal enhancement
No calvarial or skull base metastatic lesions
No dural-based masses
Chronic small vessel ischemic disease unchanged
output.impression: 1. Acute right parietal-occipital infarction, unchanged from prior study. 2. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease on dedicated metastatic protocol MRI. The findings are entirely consistent with an ischemic stroke and not with brain metastasis.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Vascular territory distribution, restricted diffusion, no enhancement, no metastatic features
No enhancing lesions on dedicated metastatic protocol

recommended_actions: Continue stroke workup and secondary prevention
Repeat brain MRI in 3 months for oncologic surveillance per standard of care

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right posterior cerebral artery territory, cardioembolic etiology secondary to atrial fibrillation

differential.key_distinguishing: MRI shows vascular territory DWI restriction without enhancement; no ring-enhancing or nodular lesions; metastatic protocol MRI negative. The lesion conforms to a vascular territory rather than appearing as a round mass.
No enhancement on post-contrast imaging, lesion respects vascular territory boundaries, no infiltrative pattern, DWI/ADC pattern consistent with ischemia rather than tumor cellularity
No evidence of hemorrhage on any MRI sequence including SWI; DWI pattern characteristic of ischemic rather than hemorrhagic event

Stroke pattern is single-territory (right PCA) consistent with cardioembolism rather than multi-territory embolic shower seen in Trousseau syndrome; atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation provides sufficient mechanistic explanation

DWI restriction with ADC confirmation indicates true infarction rather than vasogenic edema; lesion is unilateral and conforms to vascular territory rather than bilateral parietal-occipital distribution of PRES

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke alert and perform rapid neurological assessment including NIHSS and establish last known well time
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 8, LKW approximately 22:00 previous evening (10.5 hours prior)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI (or CT head if MRI unavailable) to differentiate stroke from brain metastasis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute right parietal-occipital infarction with DWI restriction in PCA territory, no enhancement to suggest metastasis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and troponin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation confirming no therapeutic anticoagulation, anemia related to surgery/cancer, stress hyperglycemia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to identify cardiac rhythm and assess for cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response confirming cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Determine thrombolysis eligibility: patient is outside 4.5-hour window (wake-up stroke, LKW >10 hours ago) and recent major surgery within 3 weeks is a relative contraindication to IV tPA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: IV alteplase contraindicated due to recent major surgery (3 weeks) and outside standard time window

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion and plan for potential thrombectomy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right PCA P2 occlusion; no significant carotid stenosis

optimal_actions.action: Assess thrombectomy candidacy: posterior circulation LVO, but NIHSS 8 is below typical thrombectomy threshold for PCA occlusion, and time from LKW is >10 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Thrombectomy may not be indicated for PCA territory stroke with moderate deficit

optimal_actions.action: Order echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure and function and assess for additional cardioembolic risk
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dilated left atrium, mildly reduced EF, consistent with AF-related structural heart disease and high cardioembolic risk

optimal_actions.action: Plan anticoagulation strategy: coordinate with surgical team regarding timing of full anticoagulation given recent esophagectomy (3 weeks); consider bridging with therapeutic-dose enoxaparin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Apixaban appropriate for AF-related stroke prevention; coordinate with surgery for timing given recent esophagectomy

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit for monitoring, optimize rate control for atrial fibrillation, and manage vascular risk factors

critical_actions: Obtain emergent neuroimaging to differentiate acute ischemic stroke from brain metastasis given cancer history
Identify atrial fibrillation as stroke mechanism and recognize that perioperative discontinuation of anticoagulation is the precipitating factor
Correctly determine that IV tPA is contraindicated (outside time window and recent major surgery within 3 weeks)
Recognize the MRI pattern as ischemic stroke (DWI restriction in vascular territory, no enhancement) rather than brain tumor despite cancer history
Plan for anticoagulation restart with appropriate multidisciplinary coordination (neurology, surgery, oncology)
Admit to stroke unit for monitoring and secondary prevention
contraindicated_actions: Administering IV alteplase: patient is outside the 4.5-hour time window and had major surgery 3 weeks ago, which is a contraindication
Assuming the brain lesion is metastatic and initiating radiation therapy or chemotherapy without completing stroke workup
Performing stereotactic brain biopsy of what is actually an acute infarct
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase (risk of worsening ischemia in penumbral tissue)
Performing lumbar puncture without clinical indication in a patient with acute large territory stroke
Administering full-dose anticoagulation immediately without coordinating with surgical team regarding recent esophagectomy
key_reasoning_points: New focal neurological deficits (left homonymous hemianopsia, confusion, left-sided weakness) in a patient with cancer history requires urgent differentiation between stroke and brain metastasis — MRI is the key discriminating investigation

MRI demonstrates DWI restriction conforming to a vascular territory (right PCA) with ADC confirmation and absence of enhancement, which is pathognomonic for acute ischemic stroke and inconsistent with brain metastasis

Known atrial fibrillation with perioperative discontinuation of anticoagulation is a well-established and sufficient mechanism for cardioembolic stroke — this eliminates the need to invoke cancer-related hypercoagulability
IV tPA is contraindicated both because of the wake-up stroke timing (LKW >10 hours) and recent major surgery (esophagectomy 3 weeks ago)

The initial differential between stroke and brain metastasis was reasonable and reflects real clinical uncertainty that occurs in cancer patients with new neurological deficits — this mirrors the published case's diagnostic dilemma
Secondary prevention requires careful multidisciplinary coordination: anticoagulation timing must balance stroke recurrence risk against postoperative bleeding risk from recent esophagectomy
The right PCA territory distribution explains the left homonymous hemianopsia (occipital involvement) and the left-sided neglect and confusion (parietal involvement)

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - although the differential with brain metastasis adds clinical context, the MRI findings clearly demonstrate a vascular territory infarct pattern with DWI restriction and no enhancement, the ECG shows atrial fibrillation, and the perioperative discontinuation of anticoagulation provides a clear mechanistic explanation
